article.title: From Touchpoints to Transitions: A Journey-Centric Approach to Customer Analytics

editorial_activity.content:
KPIs such as bounce rates, sessions, pageviews were the foundation of a digital dashboard for nearly two decades, while in general KPIs are used according to the context of the analysis to measure and report performance, they only help us understand ‘what happened’ and not so much to understand ‘why users behave in certain ways or how business outcomes can be achieved’. As customer journeys have become increasingly complex and businesses are expected to deliver consistent performance improvements every passing quarter or year, the insights expected from data have increased in both volume and complexity. Analysts are expected to answer questions such as,

* Why did conversions drop despite an increase in traffic?

* Why do users abandon their carts even after adding shipping information?

* Why do our most loyal customers suddenly churn?

The question ‘why’ is deceptively simple and greatly influences the business and marketing strategy, but deriving meaningful insights is difficult. The aggregated metrics, KPIs, and dimension combinations that signal health often hide important behavioral nuances needed to understand and interpret the ‘why.’ The problem is not with the dashboarding platforms or the concept of KPIs. The questions business stakeholders ask have simply evolved beyond what dashboards can answer.

To effectively address business concerns and keep pace with evolving customer expectation, especially when Customer Experience (CX) has become far more fragmented across diverse channels, devices, and asynchronous interactions, the Analytics practice should evolve to look beyond traditional KPIs and structured dashboards toward a journey-centric analysis approach, I call it the Journey Explorer.

Journey Analysis as a concept is not new. Traditional analysis was focused only on ‘Progression’ with Funnel and Flow visualization, ignoring the critical causal factors of the journey, such as Friction, Detours, and Recovery. Let’s look at how applying these lenses on a simple Customer Journey on an Ecommerce site helps in getting better insights,

### **1. Progression: **It focuses on **“**Do** **users move forward”.

Image 1: [https://drive.google.com/file/d/17wvnnJux1ZXbe-dC_WdOanmgwy7NrXFQ](https://drive.google.com/file/d/17wvnnJux1ZXbe-dC_WdOanmgwy7NrXFQ/edit)

It tells us whether users are moving forward in the journey as designed or there are stages where users fall the most, signaling Friction at that journey stage, hampering users to move forward in the expected journey flow.

In the Ecommerce site analysis, this was at ‘View Item’ stage, the progression is only 66% and dropping over 30% of users in the journey, clearly there is a Friction at this stage which is affecting the user’s progression. At the same time ‘Add Shipping’ stage has the lowest Friction with only 10% of users who added their shipping information ended up not Purchasing, demonstrating high Progression rate, clear indication that designed\
experience is aligned with the user intent.

### 2. **Friction: **It focuses on “Where do Users stall?”.

Image 2: <https://drive.google.com/file/d/1LaTEHs3hbnglmwUz0t4_J6k2aJTLBOdu/view?usp=drive_link>

Unlike bounce rate, which tells us where someone left, friction analysis tells us where the user hesitated, how long they stayed stuck, and whether they attempted the step multiple times before giving up.

As we saw in our Journey Explorer analysis, friction exists at ‘View Item’ stage related to Product View on the Ecommerce site. There can be many reasons for Friction that need to be diagnosed, the diagnosis can be effectively done by applying Micro-events, Time factor and other aspects depending on the context of Analysis.

Image 3: <https://drive.google.com/file/d/1GQLBYUjfqxEf-G_ma0Y30FfI6SUJcwBN/view?usp=drive_link>

In our analysis the Friction was contributed by the errors and time taken by users to complete the action measured with Time distribution. The reason for longer time to action can be analyzed with Channel, Page, and Micro-Events at the stage. The Friction at this stage was also due to, most users are choosing ‘Add items to Wishlist’ rather than ‘Add to Cart’, although this is a favorable action it is not aligned to direct Journey Progression.

These are the actionable challenges that need to be resolved to remove Friction in user’s journey.

 

### 3. **Detour: **It focuses on “Where do Users go when they get lost?”.

Image 4: <https://drive.google.com/file/d/1GF04Q_bKkezTBLPtYRLxtqP68wLWp28Y/view?usp=drive_link>

It is perhaps one of the most underutilized insights in traditional analytics. When users are not proceeding forward in journey, they either exit the site or take a detour to some other page which is not aligned with the designed journey. Detour analysis helps in mapping those alternate paths, revealing whether the users are seeking help, second-guessing their intent, or working around a broken experience. The detour paths often expose UX gaps that no survey or heatmap would surface.

In our analysis, we see users browsing FAQ and Shipping Policy pages at the critical Checkout process, although volume is low these detours could lead to Abandoned Cart journeys.

### 4. **Recovery: **It focuses on “Do Users come back?”.

Image 5: <https://drive.google.com/file/d/1z3oXuAg9eqcA4-kjR3Qfgk-hKQ1BOPY4/view?usp=drive_link>

It helps in understanding if the users who detoured were a temporary distraction or break in the journey. Behavior of users who detoured and returned is distinctly different from the one who never left, as they demonstrated intent but got distracted. Recovery rates, analyzed alongside the detour paths give analysts a direct measure of experience resilience, how forgiving is the journey when something goes wrong?

In our analysis, only 38% of the users who detoured recovered back in the journey suggesting a need to enhance the journey experience to improve their resilience.

As an additional lens, consider filtering the Journey Explorer based on Cohorts and Audience Segments.

### 5. **Cohort based Journey Analysis:**

Journey on any website/app is not uniform across all the users, there are inherent Cohorts of users who would interact with the website/app differently, having the ability to view Journey Exploration across all users and when needed filtering by predefined cohorts give a better understanding of the change in Journey Factors and Performance across Cohorts.

To get additional perspectives and insights on each of the focus areas seen above, we can look at stage specific contributors such as,

* **Pages or Top paths:** It helps in understanding what are the top contributing pages at the stage, these pages play critical influencing role on user’s action, any page which is expected and not present in the list should be analyzed and optimized to improve the performance.

Image 6: <https://drive.google.com/file/d/1JZzUCXxIelFJHiy1NmYuaWSLV6n-YJ42/view?usp=drive_link>

* **Channels:** The top traffic source view at the journey stage helps in understanding if the volumes at a particular journey stage is greatly influenced by internal site navigation and finding methods or driven by the marketing campaigns

Image 7: <https://drive.google.com/file/d/1x4rQ2eJj3Uzq3XabePaitOcUsHCfYdHf/view?usp=drive_link>

* **Micro-events:** As we already saw at ‘View Item’ stage while understanding Friction, Micro-events are greatly helpful in understanding if the actions performed by the users are aligned directly to the Journey progression or contributing to Friction although still favorable, for instance the ‘Add to Wishlist’ action instead of ‘Add to Cart’ action which drives the real business goal.

KPIs such as bounce rates, sessions, pageviews were the foundation of a digital dashboard for nearly two decades, while in general KPIs are used according to the context of the analysis to measure and report performance, they only help us understand ‘what happened’ and not so much to understand ‘why users behave in certain ways or how business outcomes can be achieved’. As customer journeys have become increasingly complex and businesses are expected to deliver consistent performance improvements every passing quarter or year, the insights expected from data have increased in both volume and complexity. Analysts are expected to answer questions such as,

* Why do users abandon their carts even after adding shipping information?

* Why do our most loyal customers suddenly churn?

The question ‘why’ is deceptively simple and greatly influences the business and marketing strategy, but deriving meaningful insights is difficult. The aggregated metrics, KPIs, and dimension combinations that signal health often hide important behavioral nuances needed to understand and interpret the ‘why.’ The problem is not with the dashboarding platforms or the concept of KPIs. The questions business stakeholders ask have simply evolved beyond what dashboards can answer.

To effectively address business concerns and keep pace with evolving customer expectation, especially when Customer Experience (CX) has become far more fragmented across diverse channels, devices, and asynchronous interactions, the Analytics practice should evolve to look beyond traditional KPIs and structured dashboards toward a journey-centric analysis approach, I call it the Journey Explorer.

experience is aligned with the user intent.

Unlike bounce rate, which tells us where someone left, friction analysis tells us where the user hesitated, how long they stayed stuck, and whether they attempted the step multiple times before giving up.

Image 3: <https://drive.google.com/file/d/1GQLBYUjfqxEf-G_ma0Y30FfI6SUJcwBN/view?usp=drive_link>

In our analysis the Friction was contributed by the errors and time taken by users to complete the action measured with Time distribution. The reason for longer time to action can be analyzed with Channel, Page, and Micro-Events at the stage. The Friction at this stage was also due to, most users are choosing ‘Add items to Wishlist’ rather than ‘Add to Cart’, although this is a favorable action it is not aligned to direct Journey Progression.

Image 4: <https://drive.google.com/file/d/1GF04Q_bKkezTBLPtYRLxtqP68wLWp28Y/view?usp=drive_link>

It is perhaps one of the most underutilized insights in traditional analytics. When users are not proceeding forward in journey, they either exit the site or take a detour to some other page which is not aligned with the designed journey. In our analysis, we see users browsing FAQ and Shipping Policy pages at the critical Checkout process, although volume is low these detours could lead to Abandoned Cart journeys.

Image 5: <https://drive.google.com/file/d/1z3oXuAg9eqcA4-kjR3Qfgk-hKQ1BOPY4/view?usp=drive_link>

It helps in understanding if the users who detoured were a temporary distraction or break in the journey. Behavior of users who detoured and returned is distinctly different from the one who never left, as they demonstrated intent but got distracted. To get additional perspectives and insights on each of the focus areas seen above, we can look at stage specific contributors such as,

* **Pages or Top paths:** It helps in understanding what are the top contributing pages at the stage, these pages play critical influencing role on user’s action, any page which is expected and not present in the list should be analyzed and optimized to improve the performance.

Image 6: <https://drive.google.com/file/d/1JZzUCXxIelFJHiy1NmYuaWSLV6n-YJ42/view?usp=drive_link>

* **Channels:** The top traffic source view at the journey stage helps in understanding if the volumes at a particular journey stage is greatly influenced by internal site navigation and finding methods or driven by the marketing campaigns

Image 7: <https://drive.google.com/file/d/1x4rQ2eJj3Uzq3XabePaitOcUsHCfYdHf/view?usp=drive_link>

* **Micro-events:** As we already saw at ‘View Item’ stage while understanding Friction, Micro-events are greatly helpful in understanding if the actions performed by the users are aligned directly to the Journey progression or contributing to Friction although still favorable, for instance the ‘Add to Wishlist’ action instead of ‘Add to Cart’ action which drives the real business goal.

### Conclusion

Journey Explorer can represent a meaningful approach for analysts to conduct customer journey analysis. By shifting the unit of analysis from just pages to transitions in the journey, it brings into focus what aggregate metrics have obscured, the actual experience of the users moving through the journey, making decisions, hitting walls, taking detours, and sometimes recovering to find their way back.

No single lens makes the Journey Explorer powerful. It is the combination of lenses along with cohorts and their contributing factors. Progression establishes the baseline. Friction pinpoints where intervention is needed. Detour reveals the path users take when the designed journey fails them. Recovery measures how well the experience holds under real-world conditions. Cohort filtering ensures these insights are specific rather than averaged across all users, because behavior that looks uniform at aggregate data level is rarely uniform in practice.

For analysts, Journey Explorer is not a replacement for dashboards, it is an additional layer that answers the questions dashboards sometimes surface but cannot resolve independently. When a KPI drops, Journey Explorer tells you where in the journey that drop originated, what did users do instead, and whether the experience recovered. That is the difference between monitoring performance and understanding the journey.

KPIs such as bounce rates, sessions, pageviews were the foundation of a digital dashboard for nearly two decades, while in general KPIs are used according to the context of the analysis to measure and report performance, they only help us understand ‘what happened’ and not so much to understand ‘why users behave in certain ways or how business outcomes can be achieved’. When a KPI drops, Journey Explorer tells you where in the journey that drop originated, what did users do instead, and whether the experience recovered. That is the difference between monitoring performance and understanding the journey.

editorial_activity.comment: Improve the sentence structure for the last paragraph in the conclusion.

editorial_activity.content:
KPIs such as bounce rates, sessions, pageviews were the foundation of a digital dashboard for nearly two decades, while in general KPIs are used according to the context of the analysis to measure and report performance, they only help us understand ‘what happened’ and not so much to understand ‘why users behave in certain ways or how business outcomes can be achieved’. As customer journeys have become increasingly complex and businesses are expected to deliver consistent performance improvements every passing quarter or year, the insights expected from data have increased in both volume and complexity. Analysts are expected to answer questions such as,

* Why did conversions drop despite an increase in traffic?

* Why do users abandon their carts even after adding shipping information?

* Why do our most loyal customers suddenly churn?

The question ‘why’ is deceptively simple and greatly influences the business and marketing strategy, but deriving meaningful insights is difficult. The aggregated metrics, KPIs, and dimension combinations that signal health often hide important behavioral nuances needed to understand and interpret the ‘why.’ The problem is not with the dashboarding platforms or the concept of KPIs. The questions business stakeholders ask have simply evolved beyond what dashboards can answer.

To effectively address business concerns and keep pace with evolving customer expectation, especially when Customer Experience (CX) has become far more fragmented across diverse channels, devices, and asynchronous interactions, the Analytics practice should evolve to look beyond traditional KPIs and structured dashboards toward a journey-centric analysis approach, I call it the Journey Explorer.

Journey Analysis as a concept is not new. Traditional analysis was focused only on ‘Progression’ with Funnel and Flow visualization, ignoring the critical causal factors of the journey, such as Friction, Detours, and Recovery. Let’s look at how applying these lenses on a simple Customer Journey on an Ecommerce site helps in getting better insights,

### **1. Progression: **It focuses on **“**Do** **users move forward”.

Image 1: [https://drive.google.com/file/d/17wvnnJux1ZXbe-dC_WdOanmgwy7NrXFQ](https://drive.google.com/file/d/17wvnnJux1ZXbe-dC_WdOanmgwy7NrXFQ/edit)

It tells us whether users are moving forward in the journey as designed or there are stages where users fall the most, signaling Friction at that journey stage, hampering users to move forward in the expected journey flow.

In the Ecommerce site analysis, this was at ‘View Item’ stage, the progression is only 66% and dropping over 30% of users in the journey, clearly there is a Friction at this stage which is affecting the user’s progression. At the same time ‘Add Shipping’ stage has the lowest Friction with only 10% of users who added their shipping information ended up not Purchasing, demonstrating high Progression rate, clear indication that designed experience is aligned with the user intent.

### 2. **Friction: **It focuses on “Where do Users stall?”.

Image 2: <https://drive.google.com/file/d/1LaTEHs3hbnglmwUz0t4_J6k2aJTLBOdu/view?usp=drive_link>

Unlike bounce rate, which tells us where someone left, friction analysis tells us where the user hesitated, how long they stayed stuck, and whether they attempted the step multiple times before giving up.

As we saw in our Journey Explorer analysis, friction exists at ‘View Item’ stage related to Product View on the Ecommerce site. There can be many reasons for Friction that need to be diagnosed, the diagnosis can be effectively done by applying Micro-events, Time factor and other aspects depending on the context of Analysis.

Image 3: <https://drive.google.com/file/d/1GQLBYUjfqxEf-G_ma0Y30FfI6SUJcwBN/view?usp=drive_link>

In our analysis the Friction was contributed by the errors and time taken by users to complete the action measured with Time distribution. The reason for longer time to action can be analyzed with Channel, Page, and Micro-Events at the stage. The Friction at this stage was also due to, most users are choosing ‘Add items to Wishlist’ rather than ‘Add to Cart’, although this is a favorable action it is not aligned to direct Journey Progression.

These are the actionable challenges that need to be resolved to remove Friction in user’s journey.

 

### 3. **Detour: **It focuses on “Where do Users go when they get lost?”.

Image 4: <https://drive.google.com/file/d/1GF04Q_bKkezTBLPtYRLxtqP68wLWp28Y/view?usp=drive_link>

It is perhaps one of the most underutilized insights in traditional analytics. When users are not proceeding forward in journey, they either exit the site or take a detour to some other page which is not aligned with the designed journey. Detour analysis helps in mapping those alternate paths, revealing whether the users are seeking help, second-guessing their intent, or working around a broken experience. The detour paths often expose UX gaps that no survey or heatmap would surface.

In our analysis, we see users browsing FAQ and Shipping Policy pages at the critical Checkout process, although volume is low these detours could lead to Abandoned Cart journeys.

### 4. **Recovery: **It focuses on “Do Users come back?”.

Image 5: <https://drive.google.com/file/d/1z3oXuAg9eqcA4-kjR3Qfgk-hKQ1BOPY4/view?usp=drive_link>

It helps in understanding if the users who detoured were a temporary distraction or break in the journey. Behavior of users who detoured and returned is distinctly different from the one who never left, as they demonstrated intent but got distracted. Recovery rates, analyzed alongside the detour paths give analysts a direct measure of experience resilience, how forgiving is the journey when something goes wrong?

In our analysis, only 38% of the users who detoured recovered back in the journey suggesting a need to enhance the journey experience to improve their resilience.

As an additional lens, consider filtering the Journey Explorer based on Cohorts and Audience Segments.

### 5. **Cohort based Journey Analysis:**

Journey on any website/app is not uniform across all the users, there are inherent Cohorts of users who would interact with the website/app differently, having the ability to view Journey Exploration across all users and when needed filtering by predefined cohorts give a better understanding of the change in Journey Factors and Performance across Cohorts.

To get additional perspectives and insights on each of the focus areas seen above, we can look at stage specific contributors such as,

* **Pages or Top paths:** It helps in understanding what are the top contributing pages at the stage, these pages play critical influencing role on user’s action, any page which is expected and not present in the list should be analyzed and optimized to improve the performance.

Image 6: <https://drive.google.com/file/d/1JZzUCXxIelFJHiy1NmYuaWSLV6n-YJ42/view?usp=drive_link>

* **Channels:** The top traffic source view at the journey stage helps in understanding if the volumes at a particular journey stage is greatly influenced by internal site navigation and finding methods or driven by the marketing campaigns

Image 7: <https://drive.google.com/file/d/1x4rQ2eJj3Uzq3XabePaitOcUsHCfYdHf/view?usp=drive_link>

* **Micro-events:** As we already saw at ‘View Item’ stage while understanding Friction, Micro-events are greatly helpful in understanding if the actions performed by the users are aligned directly to the Journey progression or contributing to Friction although still favorable, for instance the ‘Add to Wishlist’ action instead of ‘Add to Cart’ action which drives the real business goal.

### Conclusion

Journey Explorer can represent a meaningful approach for analysts to conduct customer journey analysis. By shifting the unit of analysis from just pages to transitions in the journey, it brings into focus what aggregate metrics have obscured, the actual experience of the users moving through the journey, making decisions, hitting walls, taking detours, and sometimes recovering to find their way back.\
\
No single lens makes the Journey Explorer powerful. It is the combination of lenses along with cohorts and their contributing factors. Progression establishes the baseline. Friction pinpoints where intervention is needed. Detour reveals the path users take when the designed journey fails them. Recovery measures how well the experience holds under real-world conditions. Cohort filtering ensures these insights are specific rather than averaged across all users, because behavior that looks uniform at aggregate data level is rarely uniform in practice.

For analysts, Journey Explorer is an additional layer and not a replacement for dashboards, it answers the questions the dashboards surface most of the time but cannot resolve them independently. When a KPI drops, Journey Explorer tells us where the drop originated from in the journey, what did the users do instead, and whether the experience was recovered. That is the difference between monitoring the performance and understanding the journey.

KPIs such as bounce rates, sessions, pageviews were the foundation of a digital dashboard for nearly two decades, while in general KPIs are used according to the context of the analysis to measure and report performance, they only help us understand ‘what happened’ and not so much to understand ‘why users behave in certain ways or how business outcomes can be achieved’. As customer journeys have become increasingly complex and businesses are expected to deliver consistent performance improvements every passing quarter or year, the insights expected from data have increased in both volume and complexity. Analysts are expected to answer questions such as,

* Why did conversions drop despite an increase in traffic?

* Why do users abandon their carts even after adding shipping information?

* Why do our most loyal customers suddenly churn?

The question ‘why’ is deceptively simple and greatly influences the business and marketing strategy, but deriving meaningful insights is difficult. The aggregated metrics, KPIs, and dimension combinations that signal health often hide important behavioral nuances needed to understand and interpret the ‘why.’ The problem is not with the dashboarding platforms or the concept of KPIs. The questions business stakeholders ask have simply evolved beyond what dashboards can answer.

editorial_activity.comment:
**Coherence:** 7/10
The article follows a logical structural arc from problem identification to framework introduction to component breakdown, and the four-lens structure (Progression, Friction, Detour, Recovery) gives the piece a clear skeleton. However, the opening paragraph is syntactically overloaded and disrupts early momentum; the transition into "Journey Explorer" as a named framework feels abrupt without a proper definitional anchor before the framework is deployed.

**Relevance:** 8/10
The content is tightly scoped to analytics practitioners working in digital and ecommerce contexts, and the questions posed at the outset ("Why did conversions drop despite an increase in traffic?") resonate directly with the target audience's lived professional experience. The addition of cohort filtering and micro-events elevates relevance beyond surface-level content. A brief acknowledgment of how this framework intersects with existing tools (GA4, Adobe, CDP platforms) would sharpen practitioner applicability.

**Accuracy:** 7/10
The conceptual definitions of Friction, Detour, and Recovery are analytically sound and correctly distinguished from conventional funnel metrics. The Wishlist-versus-Cart distinction as a friction signal is a genuinely accurate behavioral observation. However, the claim that "no survey or heatmap would surface" detour paths is an overstatement — session replay and heatmap tools do capture off-path navigation to some degree, and this assertion weakens analytical credibility.

**Readability:** 6/10
The prose is readable in sections but inconsistent throughout. The opening paragraph is a run-on construction that will lose readers before the framework is introduced. Capitalization of terms like Friction, Detour, Recovery, Progression, and Cohort is applied inconsistently and without explanation, creating a stylistic oddity that reads as unpolished rather than intentional. Sentence length varies appropriately in the body, but several transitions are clipped and underdeveloped.

**Originality:** 7/10
The "Journey Explorer" framing — particularly the four-lens model that extends traditional funnel analysis — represents a genuine conceptual contribution rather than a repackaging of standard analytics content. The Detour and Recovery lenses are underrepresented in mainstream analytics discourse, and naming them explicitly adds value. The article does not exhibit the hallmarks of heavy AI generation: sentence rhythm varies, the examples are specific rather than generic, and there are no filler clichés of the "in today's fast-paced world" variety. The writing reads as human-authored with some structural roughness consistent with that assessment.

**Grammar:** 5/10
This is the article's most significant technical weakness. The opening paragraph is a structurally malformed run-on. Commas are used in place of periods or semicolons in multiple instances ("...I call it the Journey Explorer" is appended without proper punctuation separation). Capitalization of common nouns (Friction, Detour, Cohort) is inconsistent across sections. Phrases such as "the one who never left" and "most users are choosing" reflect informal grammatical construction inappropriate for a professional publication. A full copy-edit pass is mandatory before publication.

**Tone:** 7/10
The article carries a practitioner's voice throughout, and the first-person ownership of the Journey Explorer framework ("I call it") lends credibility and authority. The tone does not inflate claims unnecessarily and avoids hype language. Occasional informality ("Let's look at how") is borderline for this publication's standards but does not critically undermine the professional register.

**Completeness:** 6/10
The framework is introduced and its components are explained with ecommerce examples, but the article stops short of telling analysts *how* to implement this approach. There is no guidance on data infrastructure requirements, event taxonomy design, tooling considerations, or how to handle cross-device and cross-session journeys — all of which are material concerns for practitioners attempting to apply this in practice. The Cohort section (Section 5) is noticeably thin compared to the four core lenses and reads as an afterthought. The conclusion is strong but cannot compensate for the implementation gap in the body.

**Title and Hook:** 6/10
The title is the article's strongest single element — "From Touchpoints to Transitions" is precise, evocative, and correctly signals a conceptual shift rather than a listicle. The opening paragraph, however, fails to honor the title's promise. It front-loads a dense clause about legacy KPIs without a scene-setting statement, a concrete scenario, or a tension that pulls the reader forward. The hook needs to be rebuilt as a focused, punchy opening that earns the structural argument that follows.

---

**AI-Generation Index:** 12%
**Overall:** 7/10
The article's primary strength is its conceptual framework: the four-lens Journey Explorer model is analytically coherent, meaningfully differentiated from conventional funnel analysis, and communicated with practitioner specificity. The Detour and Recovery lenses in particular represent underexplored territory that adds genuine value to the publication's audience. However, the article is held back by a grammatically deficient opening, inconsistent capitalization conventions that signal unfinished editing, an overstatement regarding heatmap limitations, and a shallow implementation section that leaves practitioners without actionable next steps. The article meets the 7/10 threshold for conditional approval but must undergo a mandatory copy-edit pass, a reconstructed opening paragraph, and an expanded Section 5 before it is suitable for publication.
Sent back to Revision to make the critical fixes suggested by Claudia, AI Editor
